title: Human Approval Boundary Packet
summary: Notes about when AI-generated operational recommendations can act automatically versus require review.

sources.title: Governance workshop
sources.body:
Participants approved automatic drafting, summarization, and artifact linking when outputs are clearly marked as generated. They rejected automatic policy changes, customer commitments, and release blocking without a human approver.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the human governance case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: Drafting, summarization, and artifact linking can be automated with generated status. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: Policy changes, customer commitments, and release blocking require human approval.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not allow automated customer commitments. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Operations incident note
sources.body:
A prior automation posted a recommendation as if it were an approved decision, causing two teams to pause rollout work unnecessarily. The incident review asks for explicit status labels: draft, recommendation, approved action, and blocked.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the human governance case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: Policy changes, customer commitments, and release blocking require human approval. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: UI status labels should distinguish drafts, recommendations, approved actions, and blocked states.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not present generated recommendations as approved decisions. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Product analytics
sources.body:
Users are most likely to accept generated briefings when the interface separates evidence, recommendation, and action controls. Acceptance drops when the recommendation and execution button appear in the same visual cluster.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the human governance case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: UI status labels should distinguish drafts, recommendations, approved actions, and blocked states. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: Recommendation and execution controls should be visually separated.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not allow automated customer commitments. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Legal review
sources.body:
Any automated message that could be interpreted as a customer commitment needs an accountable human reviewer. Legal approved internal draft summaries as long as they cannot be confused with final commitments.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the human governance case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: Recommendation and execution controls should be visually separated. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: Drafting, summarization, and artifact linking can be automated with generated status.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not present generated recommendations as approved decisions. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Reviewer calibration document
sources.body:
Reviewers use this calibration document to decide whether the briefing selected the right evidence from the packet. The expected coverage points for this case are: Drafting, summarization, and artifact linking can be automated with generated status. Policy changes, customer commitments, and release blocking require human approval. UI status labels should distinguish drafts, recommendations, approved actions, and blocked states. Recommendation and execution controls should be visually separated.

The calibration note is intentionally not a model answer. It gives the evaluator enough context to distinguish direct support from plausible synthesis. A strong briefing should still rely on the source-specific operational notes above when making claims, and it should use this document only to understand how the packet will be judged.

The repeated failure modes under review are human-approval, action-boundary. When a generated briefing cites sources broadly without connecting the claim to a concrete caveat, gate, or decision boundary, reviewers should mark the citation as weak even if the source is topically related.

sources.title: Risk and distractor document
sources.body:
This document records traps that make the packet realistic for eval work. Known traps include: Do not allow automated customer commitments. Do not present generated recommendations as approved decisions.

Several details in the packet are deliberately adjacent to the user request but not sufficient for a shipping recommendation. For example, a metric improvement should not automatically become a rollout recommendation, a faster path should not automatically become the default path, and a draft or generated recommendation should not be treated as an approved action.

The intended task is: Recommend which Briefing Genie actions can be automated and which should require human approval. A good answer should prioritize the evidence that changes the recommendation, identify caveats that block overconfident claims, and leave open questions where the packet does not contain enough information.
